stimulus: In an investigation of the problem-solving abilities of long-tailed macaques (Macaca fascicularis), researchers failed to control for the physical difficulty of the tasks they used to evaluate the monkeys. The problem-solving abilities of macaques given tasks requiring minimal dexterity, such as pushing a lever to release a treat, were judged by the same criteria as those of macaques given physically demanding tasks, such as untwisting a cap and inserting a finger to retrieve a treat. The findings of the investigation, therefore, ______blank
stem: Which choice most logically completes the text?

answerOptions.content: reveal more about the macaques’ problem-solving abilities when tackling laboratory puzzles than when tackling problems they would meet in the wild.
are useful for identifying tasks that exceed the macaques’ problem-solving abilities but not for identifying tasks they can perform successfully.
may indicate differences in problem-solving ability among the macaques even though such differences may not actually exist.
should not be taken as representative of the problem-solving abilities of any macaque species other than M. fascicularis.

rationale:
Choice C is the best answer because it most logically completes the discussion. If researchers used the same criteria to judge macaques on tasks of very different physical difficulty, then a macaque that performed poorly on a physically demanding task might have struggled with the dexterity required rather than with the underlying problem-solving challenge. Such a macaque would be scored as less cognitively capable than one that completed an easier task, even if their actual problem-solving abilities were equal. So the findings could indicate differences in problem-solving that don't truly exist.

Choice A is incorrect because the text does not contrast laboratory tasks with naturalistic ones. Choice B is incorrect because the design flaw confounds both successful and unsuccessful performance: a success on an easy task and a failure on a hard one tell us little about either ability. Choice D is incorrect because the issue is the design of the tasks, not the species; the findings cannot be reliably generalized at all, not specifically to other macaque species.
